message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The SARS-CoV-2 main protease, M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  , is critical for its replication and is an appealing target for designing anti-SARS-CoV-2 agents. In this regard, a number of assays have been developed based on its cleavage sequence preferences to monitor its activity. These include the usage of Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET)-based substrates in vitro and a FlipGFP reporter, one which fluoresces after M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  -mediated cleavage, in live cells. Here, we have engineered a pair of genetically encoded, Bioluminescence Resonance Energy Transfer (BRET)-based sensors for detecting SARS-CoV-2 M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  proteolytic activity in living host cells as well as in vitro assays. The sensors were generated by sandwiching M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  N-terminal autocleavage sites, either AVLQSGFR (short) or KTSAVLQSGFRKME (long), in between the mNeonGreen and nanoLuc proteins. Co-expression of the sensor with the M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  in live cells resulted in its cleavage in a dose- and time-dependent manner while mutation of the critical C145 residue (C145A) in M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  completely abrogated the sensor cleavage. Importantly, the BRET-based sensors displayed increased sensitivities and specificities as compared to the recently developed FlipGFP-based M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  sensor. Additionally, the sensors recapitulated the inhibition of M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  by the well-characterized pharmacological agent GC376. Further, in vitro assays with the BRET-based M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  sensors revealed a molecular crowding-mediated increase in the rate of M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  activity and a decrease in the inhibitory potential of GC376. The sensor developed here will find direct utility in studies related to drug discovery targeting the SARS-CoV-2 M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  and functional genomics application to determine the effect of sequence variation in M
                  <jats:sup>pro</jats:sup>
                  .
                </jats:p>
title: A Genetically encoded BRET-based SARS-CoV-2 Mpro protease activity sensor
